choice: Household with 1+ Persons; per-person birth year and retirement age; household-level expenses; horizon = youngest reaches life expectancy
rationale: Three binding priors converge on this answer. (1) 1306ca6a explicitly states all personal data — including 'people' — is configuration/data with nothing hard-coded, which requires Person to be a first-class model entity rather than a single baked-in joint entity. (2) b10161a1 pins RMD start age to birth year 'per current law, 73/75 by birth year,' which only makes sense if each person's birth year is tracked individually. (3) 2949f831's Scenario carries retirement age and life expectancy as inputs; in a household with two people (the original deployers are a couple, per 1306ca6a context), those must be per-person fields or the model cannot correctly time income cessation and horizon for each spouse. Household-level expenses and the youngest-person horizon follow naturally: expenses are a joint household budget, and the plan must fund the surviving spouse through their life expectancy.
